summary: Jeff Atwood advocates for a lightweight 'buddy system' approach to code review, arguing that informal peer review with a trusted colleague is highly effective yet far more practical than formal reviews or pair programming. He cites research showing code inspections catch 55-60% of defects compared to 25-45% for various forms of testing. The post challenges the institutional resistance to code reviews, noting that the best engineers actively seek out reviewers rather than viewing it as a sign of weakness. Atwood frames the coding buddy as a more appealing alternative to pair programming—you get the benefits of another set of eyes without being 'shackled to the same computer.'
key_points: A brief, informal explanation of your code to a fellow programmer is often all it takes to dramatically improve code quality
Code inspections have a 55-60% defect detection rate, significantly higher than unit testing (25%), function testing (35%), or integration testing (45%)
The best software engineers actively seek out reviewers—it's part of what makes them the best
The 'buddy system' offers the benefits of pair programming without the overhead or awkwardness of sharing a computer full-time
If you can't find a single programmer at your company worth adopting the buddy system with, you might need to change companies
money_quotes: I am continually amazed how much better my code becomes after I've had a peer look at it.
Just one brief attempt at explaining and showing my code to a fellow programmer – that's usually all it takes.
The best software engineers I have known actively sought out reviewers.
Anyone who needs his code reviewed shouldn't be getting paid as a software developer.
The average effectiveness of design and code inspections are 55 and 60 percent.
WTFs/minute is a perfectly acceptable unit of measurement to use with your coding buddy.
I think the buddy system is a far more practical way to achieve the same results.
One of the great joys of programming is not having to do it alone.
key_insight: You don't need formal processes or pair programming to get the benefits of code review—just find one trusted colleague and make showing them your code a habit before you commit.
